title: High-dimensional geometry
summary: Geometric foundations of high-dimensional statistics: covering numbers of Euclidean and sparse balls, Johnson-Lindenstrauss dimension reduction, and the restricted isometry property for random sub-Gaussian sensing matrices.

theorems.name: Covering number of the Euclidean unit ball
theorems.blurb: The unit Euclidean ball in n dimensions admits an epsilon-cover of size at most ceil(3/epsilon)^n.

theorems.name: Covering number lower bound (volumetric)
theorems.blurb: Any epsilon-cover of the unit ball must contain at least (1/epsilon)^n points, matching the upper bound up to constants.

theorems.name: Johnson-Lindenstrauss embedding (probabilistic)
theorems.blurb: A random k-by-n sub-Gaussian projection preserves all pairwise distances among N points up to (1±epsilon) distortion with high probability.

theorems.name: Restricted isometry property of sub-Gaussian matrices
theorems.blurb: An m-by-n matrix with i.i.d. isotropic sub-Gaussian rows satisfies the (s,delta) restricted isometry property with high probability once m exceeds order s·log(en/s)/delta^2.

theorems.name: RIP implies restricted eigenvalue condition
theorems.blurb: A matrix satisfying RIP with small enough constant also satisfies the restricted eigenvalue condition used to guarantee Lasso/compressed-sensing recovery.
